Why research is home turf

I'm not just designing for researchers. I am one.

Before design, I trained in social science research and writing. As a designer I've run my own studies at every company since, because I was usually the only person who would. I know the manual grind Listen automates because I have lived every hour of it: recruiting, moderating, synthesizing, and fighting to get anyone to act on the findings.

The grind

I've done the week-long version

Screeners, scheduling, discussion guides, moderation, transcript coding, synthesis decks. I know exactly which hours hurt and which judgment calls can't be skipped.

The gap

Insight is worthless until someone acts

The hardest part of research was never the interview. It's the last mile: turning findings into a decision a PM will actually make. That last mile is a design problem.

The proof

Research that redirected a roadmap

At Square, my discovery work plus a funnel analysis killed the team's favorite hypothesis and re-aimed a quarter of work. The result is on the next slide.

Insight to action · Square Checking

Quant said what. Qual said why. The roadmap changed.

Checking activation sat at 32%. The room wanted discoverability fixes. A funnel analysis showed early debit card engagement was the real activation signal, and interviews explained why: sellers didn't know a virtual card could spend immediately, and the system never talked to them. We rebuilt around the evidence, every change behind a pre-registered experiment.

32%→59%
Overall Checking activation. The compounded result of staged, measured experiments against a stated baseline.
29%→51%
First card spend within a month. The exact behavior the research said to bet on.
23%
Activation lift from Debit Card Decoupling, plus a 38% increase in virtual card adoption.

The discipline: if we can't say in advance what number should move, we're not ready to ship it.

Complexity into simplicity

Turning week-long manual work into products people barely notice.

Listen's tl;dr is turning complex consulting projects into elegant AI experiences. That transformation is the through-line of my last decade, and the best version I've shipped had almost no UI at all.

54%→69%
Bill capture completion at Square. A three-stage ladder: manual entry, then OCR extraction, then zero-UI email forwarding. Each rung removed human effort; the best interface was the one that disappeared.
52%→73%
Vendor acceptance. The counterparty never chose the product, so the flow had to earn trust in one screen: claim, verify with Plaid, get paid.
39%
Repeat patient rate at Heal, where complex clinical data became a glanceable physician briefing. CES Innovation Award; a 39% reduction in unnecessary prescriptions; $4M+ recurring net margin.
How I work now

Not just a designer. An AI builder.

I've taken my passion to grow my skills so far that I created my own app. Deckk is a link-in-bio for creators, and its core is the same shape as Listen's: unstructured input in, structured output out. Drop a flyer, AI parses the image, extracts title, date, location, and price, and builds the page. Review, publish, seconds. Testing it currently.

Design

Craft and systems

End-to-end design, design systems, and product voice from a writing background.

Build

Production code

Next.js, Supabase, Stripe, on Vercel. Cursor and Claude Code daily.

Pattern

Confidence, not certainty

Per-field confidence scores with human review before publish. Designing trust into AI extraction is a problem I've already shipped against.

deckk.me · flyer in, structured page out · tap to enlarge
Deliverables · The other half of the job

The deliverable is what the customer keeps.

At Listen, the interview is the engine, but the deliverable is what a researcher forwards to their VP. I've owned that half of the job before. At Heal I was the only designer, so investor decks and board materials came from the same desk as the product. And the brand system this deck runs on, cobalt included, is one I designed and built for myself: you're looking at the deliverable craft right now.

Investor-grade

Deliverables with money on the line

At Heal I produced the investor and board materials while shipping the product. Same hands, same week, both at the standard the audience demanded.

Brand as a system

Not a mood board, a working system

My own brand runs on a documented design system: parchment, cobalt, a type pairing, and components. Site, decks, and templates all draw from it. This deck is the proof.

Voice

Research deliverables live or die on language

A writing and social science background means the words in an insight report are a design surface I actually own, not decorate.

Outcomes, measured

Every number here had a pre-registered baseline.

32→59%
Square Checking activation, overall
29→51%
First card spend within a month
52→73%
Bill Pay vendor acceptance
~80%
Biller coverage increase, with ~23% payment volume growth
54→69%
Bill capture completion via the OCR + email-forwarding ladder
39%
Heal repeat patient rate · $4M+ recurring net margin · 39% fewer unnecessary prescriptions

Plus: CES Innovation Award and App Store App of the Day at Heal · 23% activation lift and 38% virtual card adoption increase from Debit Card Decoupling at Square.

Why Listen Labs

This role is the intersection of everything I've been building toward.

Domain

A researcher designing research tools

I've lived the manual workflow Listen automates. I can design for your users because I've been your user, screener to synthesis.

The tl;dr

Complexity into elegance is my track record

Week-long manual processes into simple flows: bill capture, vendor claims, clinical briefings. The best one ended with no UI at all.

AI patterns

I've shipped the hard AI UX problems

Extraction confidence, human review, trust in generated output. Deckk is a working study in the exact new patterns Listen needs to invent.

Founding shape

Deliverables and product, one desk

At Heal I owned product, brand, research, investor materials, and the design system alone. The two halves of this role are a shape I have already lived, at award level.

Voice

Writing is part of the craft

A research product speaks in words: questions, summaries, insights. My writing background means the product's voice is a first-class design surface, not an afterthought.

Thank you.

Happy to go deeper on anything: the research-to-roadmap story, the zero-UI capture ladder, or how I'd design Listen's next new pattern.
